title: Respecting the Law
overview: In the UK everyone—including the government—must obey the law. This principle is called the rule of law. It protects people's rights and keeps society safe and fair. Laws are made by Parliament and enforced by the police and courts. Everyone has the right to a fair trial and to be treated equally before the law.

study_sections.heading: Rule of Law
study_sections.content: Rule of law: everyone must obey law; no one above it. Criminal law deals with offences like theft, assault, murder. Civil law settles disputes between people or organisations (contracts, family, property). Police prevent crime and protect public—must themselves act within law. PCSOs assist local policing through patrol and public contact.

study_sections.heading: Courts and Justice
study_sections.content: People who break law may be arrested and charged. Have right to legal representation and fair hearing. Magistrates' courts hear minor offences; serious crimes go to Crown Court (with judge and jury). In Scotland: sheriff courts or High Court of Justiciary. Supreme Court is highest appeal court in UK. Juries (usually 12 citizens) decide guilt/innocence in serious criminal cases.

study_sections.heading: Rights and Support
study_sections.content: Terrorism and extremism are serious crimes against public safety; citizens should report suspicious activity to police. People can get legal advice from solicitors, Citizens Advice, or community law centres. Legal aid may help those who can't afford lawyer. Respecting law means knowing rights, duties, and treating others fairly.

key_facts: Parliament makes the laws and everyone in the UK must obey them under the rule of law.
Criminal law deals with offences such as theft, assault and terrorism; civil law settles disputes between individuals or organisations.
Police services work with communities to prevent crime and bring offenders to court; Police Community Support Officers assist locally.
People can report crime to the police or anonymously through Crimestoppers.
In England and Wales most criminal cases start in Magistrates’ Courts; serious cases move to the Crown Court with a jury.
In Scotland Sheriff Courts handle many cases while the High Court of Justiciary hears the most serious offences.
The Crown Prosecution Service, Crown Office and Procurator Fiscal Service, and Public Prosecution Service decide if cases should go to court.
The UK Supreme Court is the highest court of appeal and judicial independence protects fairness.
Everyone accused of a crime is presumed innocent until proven guilty and may receive legal aid if they cannot afford representation.
Preventing extremism and terrorism relies on communities reporting concerns to the authorities.
Organisations such as Citizens Advice help people understand their legal rights and responsibilities.
memory_hook: Think of the law as a well-lit street: Parliament sets the lamps, police patrol the pavements, courts judge disputes, and citizens stay safe by walking in the light and reporting dangers.

options: Everyone must obey law including government
questions.explanation: Rule of law means no one is above law.
Parliament creates laws that courts then apply.
Criminal law deals with crimes against society.
Civil law settles private disputes.
Police officers uphold law and public safety.
PCSOs help communities and report issues to police.
Magistrates handle less serious cases locally.

questions.prompt: Who decides guilt in serious cases?
questions.explanation: Jury decides verdict in Crown Court.
Supreme Court is final court of appeal.
Judges must act without political influence.

options: Person not punished unless proven guilty
questions.explanation: Everyone has right to fair trial.
Citizens Advice provides guidance on legal issues.

options: Government help for those who can't afford lawyer
questions.explanation: Legal aid ensures access to justice for everyone.
CPS reviews evidence and brings cases to court.

options: Sheriff courts & High Court of Justiciary
questions.explanation: Scotland has own legal system and courts.
Citizens should report concerns to police.

options: Using violence to influence politics or intimidate public
questions.explanation: Terrorism is serious crime against public safety.

options: Supporting ideas rejecting democracy or tolerance
questions.explanation: Extremism opposes British values of freedom and respect.
Specialist units work to prevent terrorism.
Law protects rights and ensures justice for all.
